vpc.name: VPC Name
vpc.noSubnets: No subnets exist in the VPC. Add subnets from the Node Library sidebar.

sg.title: Security Group:
sg.description: Security groups act as a virtual firewall for your container instance to control inbound and outbound traffic. By default, all traffic that is not explicitly allowed is denied.
sg.noRules: No rules configured. All traffic is blocked.

routing.title: - Routing Table
routing.description: A Routing Table contains a set of rules (called routes) that determine where network traffic from your subnet is directed. Traffic matching the destination CIDR will be forwarded to the corresponding target.
routing.noRoutes: No routes defined. Subnet traffic will be isolated.

details.title: Database Scaling & Parameters
details.desc: Configure vertical container limits and horizontal database replication parameters.
details.scaleDesc: Replicas scale reads. Partitions divide large datasets (e.g. users) horizontally into sub-tables (p0 to p4) for optimized search performance.
details.topologyDesc: Replication uses log-shipping. Replicas act as read-only copies. Primary failures stop writes, but read queries remain available on active replicas.

simulation.title: Database Traffic & Failover Dashboard
simulation.desc: Generate traffic and test database high-availability scenarios.

explorer.title: Inspect tables and schema:
explorer.initializing: Database server is initializing... This may take a few seconds.

shell.emptyOutput: No output. Execute a query to see result tables.

details.title: Cache Node Details
details.desc: In-memory key-value cache backed by a real Redis container. Use the Key Explorer and Shell to inspect and manipulate data.

explorer.noKeys: No keys found. Use the Shell to add some.

shell.emptyOutput: No output. Run a command to see the result.

details.title: NoSQL Database Configuration
details.desc: Manage vertical resources and partition horizontal database shards.
details.scaleDesc: Shards partition collections horizontally using hash keys. Replicas provide high availability and read scalability for each shard partition.
details.topologyDesc: MongoDB sharding uses mongos as the query router. Each shard can be a replica set. Replicas synchronize data asynchronously via oplog tailing.

simulation.title: MongoDB Sharding Router Simulation
simulation.desc: Observe request hashing and eventual consistency secondary replication lag.

explorer.initializing: Database server is initializing... This may take a few seconds.

shell.placeholder: Write your MongoDB commands here (e.g. db.users.find())
shell.emptyOutput: No output. Execute a query to see result documents.

details.title: Gateway Specifications (Cloud Simulator)
details.infoCloud: Cloud Standard: In real clouds (AWS, GCP, Azure), NAT Gateways are fully managed services. Users cannot SSH or launch a terminal session on them. They operate purely as serverless routing assets.
details.infoPing: No Direct Pings: Real managed NAT Gateways do not respond to direct pings or port connections themselves. They will drop any direct traffic destined for their IP addresses, but they will successfully forward transit traffic (like pings from a private subnet server to 8.8.8.8).

explain.title: What is a NAT Gateway?
explain.para: A NAT (Network Address Translation) Gateway allows instances in a private subnet to connect to services outside your VPC (like the public internet or external APIs), but prevents external services from initiating unsolicited inbound connections with those private instances.
explain.li1: One-Way Access: Keeps instances hidden and protected from direct internet attacks while allowing them to download updates/packages.
explain.li2: Source Address Translation: Replaces the client instance's private IP with the NAT Gateway's public IP before sending the packet to the internet.
explain.li3: Stateful Routing: Remembers the outgoing connection so it can correctly return response packets back to the original client.

guide.title: Step-by-Step Configuration Guide
guide.step1Desc: Always deploy your NAT Gateway node in a Public Subnet (one that has an active internet pathway).
guide.step2Desc: In the Public Subnet routing table, verify there is an outbound rule: 0.0.0.0/0 ➡️ target igw.
guide.step3Desc: Open the Private Subnet's routing table modal. Add a new route rule: 0.0.0.0/0 ➡️ target
guide.step4Desc: Open the terminal of any client server in your Private Subnet. Run ping -c 3 1.1.1.1. The traffic will route through your NAT Gateway!

cheatsheet.title: VPC Architecture Cheat Sheet
cheatsheet.row1Col3: Bridges public subnets to public internet directly.
cheatsheet.row2Col3: Acts as middleman for private subnets. Needs an attached EIP.
cheatsheet.row3Col3: Can only access internet if routed through a NAT Gateway.

details.title: Load Balancer Specifications
details.algoLeastConn: Least Connections (Route to target with fewest connections)
details.noTargets: No targetable Ubuntu servers found in the network. Please create at least one Ubuntu server node.
details.noRules: No path routing rules configured. Defaults to routing all traffic (/) to all selected targets.
details.infoBoundary: Managed Platform Boundary: Application Load Balancers are fully managed by the orchestration platform. Direct SSH terminal access is disabled.

explain.title: Understanding Load Balancing
explain.para: An Application Load Balancer (ALB) serves as a single entry point for client traffic. It acts as a reverse proxy, distributing incoming HTTP/HTTPS requests across multiple target servers in your backend fleet.
explain.li1: High Availability: Spreads load across multiple nodes so that if one server fails, others continue serving requests.
explain.li2: Scalability: Allows scaling the application capacity horizontally by simply registering new target servers.
explain.li3: Traffic Optimization: Ensures that no single server becomes a performance bottleneck.
explain.algo1: Round Robin: Requests are forwarded to targets sequentially. Ideal when target capacities are uniform.
explain.algo2: Least Connections: Routes traffic to the target with the fewest active connections. Optimal for longer-running requests.

guide.title: Inbound Traffic Flow
guide.para: The Application Load Balancer functions inside your VPC on the public subnet. Here is how traffic traverses the network:
guide.step1Desc: The client curls or browses to the load balancer's public entrypoint:
guide.step2Desc: The Load Balancer security group evaluates the inbound request. Ensure you have an HTTP (Port 80) rule permitting traffic from 0.0.0.0/0!
guide.step3Desc: The Nginx server picks a target from your active target group according to the selected algorithm (Round Robin / Least Conn) and forwards the HTTP request to the target's IP address on port 80.

cheatsheet.title: Testing Load Balancing Locally
cheatsheet.para: To observe traffic balancing in action, follow this testing procedure:
cheatsheet.step1Title: Start a web server on target nodes:
cheatsheet.step1Desc: Open a terminal on your target Ubuntu servers and start a simple web listener. For instance:
cheatsheet.step1Note: Create a file index.html with a unique label on each target server so you can distinguish which server is replying!
cheatsheet.step2Desc: Ensure the Load Balancer node has a Security Group rule allowing TCP port 80 from 0.0.0.0/0.
cheatsheet.step3Title: Send requests to the Load Balancer:
cheatsheet.step3Desc: From your local computer terminal, run curl requests repeatedly to see Nginx cycle between targets:
